Congratulations, you guys survived the most painful presentation that anyone could ever possibly give, which is search engine stuff. So I hope it was good for you guys. That's always the hard one to teach. But it's powerful, so I want to make sure we talked about it during today. Okay, so next thing we're going to talk about, and Kurt's actually going to help me a lot in this presentation because he does a lot more of this than I do, but when I first kind of got in this whole internet business and I started learning about the internet, I had a guy that I interviewed. Some of you guys have probably heard that interview. He was a guy named Vince James, and Vince made $100 million in 23 months selling supplements through direct mail. And it was amazing, so I interviewed him for six hours asking him how he did it and kind of going through the whole process. And some of you guys have probably, who's ever heard that interview? Yeah, I was like, Brad knew this whole industry, I was scared to death, I was like so shy on the phone. So you ever heard it, that's why I was like, okay, so, I was so scared. But it was amazing, I learned so much from him and from what he was doing. And one really interesting thing he talked about at the very beginning of the interview, that didn't make hardly any sense to me at the time, it now makes perfect sense, he said, the one problem I have with the internet, he says, I feel like I'm in a Lamborghini on a dead end road. He said, I can get in there and also I can drive a bunch of traffic, make a bunch of money, but he's like, I always hit these plateaus where I can't scale things and I can't roll things out. And remember he said that, again, it didn't really make sense to me because I hadn't ever blown up anything to the point where I kind of scaled out. And he said, for him, he liked was offline stuff because you can go into TV and radio and magazines and direct mail and you can scale things infinitely high. Whereas internet you kind of plateau after a little while. And so I always believed that after I kind of started growing my business, I kind of saw that that was very true, there was always plateaus. And I believed that until about a year ago, the very first time I started learning about ad networks and how big ad networks work. And that's what this whole presentation is going to be about, is talking about what ad networks are. Ad networks give you the ability, if you can get an offer that converts and you plug into an ad network, you can literally do five, 10, 15,000 sales a day every single day for as long as you keep paying for the media if you can get the numbers to work. So that's why what I'm going to be talking about and what Kurt's going to be sharing, these things are very, very powerful because there's so much traffic, but there's some things you need to understand about it as well. Most of these sites are mass media, so a very niche specific offer is not going to convert in it, but if you have a mass media site or a general interest site, so I can make money or lose weight or something that would appeal to the majority of people, this is where these types of sites a lot of times work very, very good. So I'm going to kind of preface a couple of things just to kind of lay some of the groundwork and then Kurt's going to come up and share some of his strategies. He's been doing a lot more of this than we have. We actually have a guy that's doing this for us in our company, so it doesn't work for me. He's just a guy who does this. We hired to do it because he's brilliant. In fact, on page 74, 75, and 76, and 77, and 78, he sent me, he sent all of us a list of four or five pages of all the different ad networks that he uses right now, so this is a huge resource for all you guys. I think it's like 100 and something different ad networks in here. So again, we're going to talk more about that, but I want to talk about the three core ad networks that I've had some experience with. The first one is Polt360, second one is Adblade, and third one is Adsonar. The way these companies work is they basically go into sites that get a lot of traffic, like hundreds of millions, if not billions of visitors every single day, and they go and they buy big, huge blocks of media on these sites. For example, like MSNBC or Fox News or things like that. They'll go and they'll buy this section, and they'll buy it at one price, so they'll be spending whatever. They'll spend a million dollars a day on those ads, and they come back and they resell it to us for a million and a half dollars. But because they're buying so much bulk, so much stuff, they get it at a huge discount, so it becomes really affordable for people like us. And there's sites in all sorts of different places. In fact, one of the resources in here, we're in Mexico with Mike Geary, and Mike Geary, his product's the Truth About Abs, and he told me that he just found this adult ad network that goes off all these porno sites. He said, surprisingly enough, he's like, people that are on porno sites want to buy how to get a six-pack product. He's ever getting tons of traffic from these porno sites. And one of the resources is actually like a porno affiliate network. Now, I've never used that. We actually, I own OvercomePanography.com. That's one of my companies, and we have a whole product teach people how to overcome pornography addiction. So we're actually going to be going in those ad networks and trying to be buying pornography ads, anti-pornography ads on the pornography sites. I have no idea if they're going to let us do it or not. But anyway, but again, in that ad network alone, they serve up over a billion impressions per day just in that one ad network. So you see, if you can get something to kind of work, there is unlimited types of traffic. A billion people a day can be seeing your ads. Obviously, if you can make sure the metrics work. Mike was also telling us about, he goes and buys a lot of ads like on the homepage at MSN.com. And he says, they'll do that. They'll get 100,000 visitors in a day. But they're spending $30, $40, $50 grand on an ad. And he says that, he was telling me that you have to really time it because he's like, if you place an ad on the day that they killed Osama bin Laden, he's like, you're screwed because everyone's going there to read Osama bin Laden. Your ads being showed billions of times, and no one's clicking on your thing because they don't want everything else. You just lost 50 grand. So it's all about, for those things like timing out what are the major news stories and what's not happening and when are people going to be kind of bored as opposed to reading these in depth things. And so it's kind of crazy because you can lose your shirts really fast or you can make a billion dollars really fast. So it's kind of like one of those things. So anyway, so there's kind of some preface about ad networks. Now kind of just thinking about what these sites look like. So let's go to page 72. The first one I'm talking about here is Pulse 360. This is what their ads look like. If you notice there's a little image and some text. It looks kind of like Facebook ads, very similar to Facebook ads. Pulse 360, some of the sites they buy their media on. MSNBC.com, USA Today.com, MSN.com. One cool thing about Pulse 360 is they have wild card features. That's why you've probably seen ads that are like, Boise Ma makes $300 at home or Chicago, wherever you're at, it pulls in your city, pulls in the wild card of where you live at. So you can target things like that. And that's kind of how Pulse 360 works. You flip the page over to page 73, add Sonar's another ad network. This one's owned by AOL. So they're on all the AOL network of sites. So AOL, TMZ, ESPN. They get over 13 billion impressions per month. Again, these are more text ads. You can see what their ads look like there on page 73. Same kind of thing. And then Adblade is another one. They're on USA Today, MSNBC, Fox News as well. And theirs are more similar to Facebook with the image and also the ad as well. Now again, all these ad networks are different. There's different rules on all of them. I'm actually going to give him my mic and I'm going to run mic for questions while he's talking that way. Okay, so just to put the mic on first. All right, sound okay? All right, so really, I mean, I got into the whole media buy inside of it because I got kicked out of Google. Not really kicked out of Google. That's a harsh word. But I used to buy all kinds of ad words traffic. Actually, I have all kinds of courses on ad words and things like that. But in 2010, we spent about $700,000 to $900,000 on Google ad traffic and sending it to a guy that I was partnered with. And he had a little project where he sold, surprisingly enough, a course through a webinar. It wasn't an automated webinar. It wasn't a good process. But he made about $5 or $6 million in 2010 off of the 700,000 or so that we spent on on AdWords. And the crazy thing is, you think if you're spending that much, they would, you know, give you like a customer support rep and stuff like that. And they never did. And so eventually, it just became such a hassle that we started messing around with the ad networks more. And so when you deal with ad networks, there's there's kind of different types of ad networks. So if you go to the page, let's see, 70. 70. Let's see, where are the words that list like 74? Okay. So there's text ad networks. And then there's display ad networks. If you're just getting started, the text ad networks are infinitely easier to get started because most of you already have, you know, 25 or 40 word description, right? Or you've run Facebook ads or you've run paper click ads. So the number one thing I would actually start with is actually MSN AdCenter. There's it's about a third the traffic of AdWords, but they convert to the higher rate. So like last month, I think I made about 20,000 in sales off of MSN AdCenter traffic. They have they actually do have a content network on MSN that performs like crazy. I don't know. I can't figure out where the traffic is coming from, why it works, but it converts like crazy. So I'm going to try MSN first and then where's it? Where do we have the list of like Quigo and Adsonar? Okay. Yeah. So and then next on that list is, you know, the text ones, which are all text based, which is like industry brains, AOL sponsored links, which used to be called Adsonar. They all change names like four or five times. So if it sounds like they all sound the same, it's because they're usually they are the same. So then once you get all and really when it comes to media buying and ad networks, you just have to spend the time to do it. Either you yourself or you have to find someone in your company that spends the time. A lot of it is just, you know, I force myself every week. I got to set up three new advertising sources because really that's all it comes down to in this game. If you can get enough traffic and the traffic converts profitably, the only thing that's holding you back is getting more traffic. You know, we talked to people like Mike Geary, who has the truth about abs book. Who would have thought that you could sell millions and millions of copies of that book? It's an ebook about how to get a six pack and he runs it everywhere. You know, a lot of times we think our stuff won't work on as broad of a market as it does. You know, we constantly think, oh, well, everybody's already seen my ad. Well, that's not the case. Internet's a big, big place. So then after you do all the text ad networks, right? Then you have, let's turn to page 75, then you have PPV networks. So these are all slightly different kinds of ads, right? The one that's easiest for most people to understand is the text ad networks. Like, you know, it's almost exactly like a Google AdWords ad. If you look at the ad sonar ad blade, they're all basically the same. PPV is a little bit different. That's pay per view or CPV. Those are quasi, it's kind of a little bit grey hat. Those are people who have installed a game or a video or something on their computer that allows that network to serve them ads. So on this list, the one that I use the most is traffic vans. The one we use the second most is lead impact. And then third would be the media traffic. But PPV traffic is different than it's not, they're just seeing a little text ad, right? And clicking on your ad. They're on some random site, right? And they get a pop-up, they're on your competitor site, they get another window that completely covers their screen and it's your offer. So it has to be something a little bit more concise, it has to be something a little bit more generic. But we've had tremendous success with traffic vans. I've probably spent, in the last year, probably $20,000 on traffic vans. Some of the traffic vans, it's a little hard to get them to allow you to advertise. They're pretty selective. You have to basically deposit $500 or $1,000, you know, that you use in advertising, but that's kind of their gateway. And then once you get those all set up, then you have the, then you can get into the display ad networks. And display ad networks, that's banner ads, right? So one site you guys might want to check out, does anybody have trouble in their business just getting graphics done? You know, just getting banners and headers and all that kind of stuff? Well, I do. I mean, I have an assistant that she does all my phone work and follow-up and customer support and stuff like that. But then there's me and then I have a couple of outsourcers from the Philippines, but none of us are really great at graphics. And so it was always one of those hangups that we had. So we used to go to places like Odesk, are you guys all familiar with Odesk? Odesk or 99 Designs, if you want top quality, top shelf work, go to 99 Designs and you start a contest. You say, I'm going to pay $500 for whoever designs me the best, whatever, blog theme or banner ad package or whatever it is you're trying to design, right? And then people compete for your business. And you select the winner and then you pay the winner. But that can get a little bit on the expensive side, you know, $500 to $2,000. Another way, easy way to get stuff done is just go to places like $20banners.com and for $20, they'll give you a whole set of the seven major banner ad sizes. Or actually, I use a service called Unlimited Web Graphics. I just started using the service and it's one of those lifesaver type of things. You know, Mark Joiner talks about this all the time in his Sympyology course. Have you guys ever gone through Sympyology? If you haven't, you need to go through it. It's free, right? The 101 is still free. Just go through this Sympyology course and he talks about how we have all these blocks in our life, things that impetus from getting things done. And so often we just consider that part of life. Oh, yeah, I didn't get anything done today because my secretary came in and asked me 500 questions all day. Well, Joiner in his Sympyology course talked about, well, you have to put things that prevent that from happening. You have to have a lock on your door or a sign on the outside of your door says, do not bother me between ours or this and this. Well, in your web business, you're going to have exactly that same thing happen. You're going to have these things that hold you up. How many of you have things in your business that are holding you up? We all do, right? It's different things for every person. But you need to analyze what those are and figure out how you can get rid of those. So one of mine, like up until a couple of months ago was, well, just getting graphics done, you know, in a timely fashion like overnight or the next day, that sort of thing. So if you go to unlimited web graphics, and I'm sure there's probably other people that offer it, this lady offers a service for 147 bucks a month. She'll do banners, logos, whatever you, any kind of graphics you want done, she'll do. You can have two open projects at a time. And she, her turnaround is usually 48 hours of 72, 48 to 72 hours. So the awesome thing is, they have, you know, you think of a, I need another banner ad set. You go in, give her some ideas and 48 hours later, here's your banner ad set. And you know, 140 bucks a month. So you got to look for things like that. So when you get into the banner ad networks, that's why I mentioned banner ads. You're going to have to get a bunch of banner ads sets created. So when we talk about banner ads, there's seven major sizes, right? And I can't even name off the seven major sizes. But if you go to any site, you know, there's the normal banner ad, the skinny one, 468 by 60. But the best sizes are the, you know, 250, the 300 by 250 and 250 by 250, the square looking ones, and then also the leaderboard ones. But when you get banner ads created, you might as well just have them all created because you're going to use them all in some form or another. So what all we do is we find banner ads that we like just by surfing the internet. You know, we use a research account that I set up on Gmail. I've subscribed to a gazillion lists like we talked about. And then when I go to those sites or if I'm browsing the web and I see a banner that I like that captures my attention, I actually save it into Evernote. You guys aren't using Evernote. I highly recommend it. A free service, free piece of software that is unbelievable. Change your life, really. Save into Evernote and then once a week or so send that off to your banner ad designer. Say, hey, can you create me a set of banners that look like this? And then the next week set of banners that look like this. Because when you're talking about display advertising and banners, you got to have lots of them created. We're talking at least three or four sets of banners. So, I mean, it's not expensive. It's just one of those hurdles that's in the way. I think the display advertising, you know, using banner ads is, I can't believe that more people don't talk about it because it's everywhere on the web. It's billions and billions of dollars that are moved and add inventory. It's the biggest source of inventory, but yet most people kind of brush past and say, oh, well, banner ads don't work. Banner ads do work. They work like crazy. So, when you're dealing with banner ads, there's different kinds of networks as well. There's self-serve versus, you know, not self-serve where they kind of do it for you. One place that you're going to want to go is Ad Shuffle. I don't know if it's written down in here. Is it? Okay. Circle Ad Shuffle because Ad Shuffle will give you a free... 74. 74? Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Ad Shuffle will give you a free... Display server, right? What's a media server? Because when you deal with banner ads, it's not like you're dealing with simple text. You know, you're giving somebody a text file and they're running it in their network. With banner ads, you typically upload it to a banner ad server, right? And Ad Shuffle will give you one that you can use for free. You can have software that you install on your own server. There's third-party servers. Just to start out, I would just use Ad Shuffle because they let you use it for free. You upload all your banners to there, and then when you go buy advertising sources, when they ask for your links, you give them those links. And so it allows for all kinds of optimization and tweaking and testing. Because the awesome thing that the revolution that's happening right now in banner advertising is, how many of you are familiar with how Google AdWords works, right? Started out in 2002 as an extremely simple system. Basically, if you were willing to bid more than the next guy, you were on number one. He was number two, right? Now it's an extremely complicated, quality score driven. There's a lot of factors that go into it. Well, just in the last few years, that same revolution has started happening even before display advertising. So for example, I would circle where it says, does anybody see where it says traffic? And it spells with a Q, T-R-A-F-F-I-Q. I would circle that one as well. What page is that on? Bottom of the 74, about 7M from the bottom. Okay, yep. Because companies like traffic, usually when we think about buying banners, we're thinking in the normal sequential way. We go, I'm going to run my ad on the site. I'm going to pay on a CPM or cost per click basis. They're going to send me a bill, and it either works or it doesn't. Well, now companies like traffic are conglomerating all these different places where your ads are served, and they're using artificial intelligence to predict and to optimize your campaign. So if you go into traffic, instead of saying, well, I want to pay $7 CPM, I'm willing to pay up to $2.50 a click, right? You go the next step, you say, well, what I'm really willing to pay for is I'm really willing to pay for $25 to get someone to fill in my registration form and attend my webinar. Right? So you're one step up the food chain, right? Because I'm willing to spend $25 to get a registration to my webinar all day long, right? I don't, like, Russell kept talking about CPM. I don't care what the CPM. I'm concerned about the cost per click. Well, now in the display ad networks, we're moving a couple steps up from that. I don't cost, how much it costs per click, how much does it cost me to get someone to register for my webinar, right? So essentially, you can turn the display networks into your biggest affiliates, right? You basically tell them, well, send me as much traffic as you can get as long as I'm only paying $20, $25 to get a registration. Does that make sense to you? Does it make sense? Do you think that could help you in your business? Okay, that's a trial closed. Sorry, I learned that from Russell. I'm not as good at Russell. I'm still working on it. But was that one okay? Let's see. So we talked about media servers. We talked about the self-serve networks. Oh, okay, here's another one. Go to page 77, the retargeting. Raise your hand if you don't know what retargeting is. Okay, raise your hand if you already use retargeting. Okay, let's have a simple explanation for people who don't understand what retargeting is. Okay, so if I have an ad source and I get a thousand clicks, well, let's make it easier for the numbers, I get a hundred clicks to my registration page, right? How many of those people are going to register? Well, it depends on how good the page is, but if it's from email traffic, about 30 to 40%. So I'm going to get 30 or 40 registrations. And those people are now in my funnel. They get emails to attend the webinar and the SMS and all that kind of stuff through my automated webinar. So what happens to the 60 or 50 or 60 people who came to my site and didn't register? Stock. Right, I would like to stock them, right, and follow them around and find out, well, why didn't you register for my webinar? Come on, it's a good deal. It's free. So that's what retargeting is. It targets people who come to your website, a click that you paid for, but then didn't take the action that you wanted. So you can use services like retargeter.com. The ones that I use are retargeter.com, fetchback.com, and then through Google themselves, through Google retargeting. But retargeting is a huge, huge, huge value, because when you're paying for traffic, and investing, if I can get traffic for $7 or $10 CPM, that's $7 or $10 for each thousand times my ad is viewed, I can make money from that, right? Well, now imagine, okay, so there's a paying $7 to $10 per thousand for traffic that hasn't been to my website. What if I could pay $7 to $10 per thousand for people who have already been to my website and didn't fill in the registration form? Do you see how that's kind of an apples to oranges comparison? Of course I'm going to pay that. They've already been to my website, because I can now show them ads that are very targeted and speak to exactly what I... What do I know about them? Well, they've already been to my website. So you'll see this if you go to... If you do any sort of things in internet marketing or in, you know, finance type of companies or any of those... Any companies are good at direct response. You'll notice for the... If you go to a site like, I don't know, blinds.com, and you go look at blueblinds on blinds.com, and they go to ESPN or AOL, you're going to see banner ads from blinds.com that says, hey, we have a special deal on blue blinds. Why don't you come back and buy those blue blinds you were just looking at? Not quite that creepy, but basically. I mean, they're pretty targeted. Yeah, go ahead. Tell you how screwy it is. We ran one of these. I was taking my life to the point of trying to rent a place on Lake Como. And I go in and I start looking for places in Lake Como, and my own ads are showing up when I'm looking for a place in Lake Como. Lake Como, Italy. But they follow you everywhere. Yep. And so the way that that works is, that's how most people think about retargeting. It's okay. The obvious thing is, somebody comes to my site, doesn't buy. I'm going to have these banner ads follow them around until they come back to my site and do buy. Well, what about the next step? What about after they register for your webinar? For most people, there's a gap in time from the time they register until the time they watch the webinar, right? You could build a second sublist of those people. And the banner ads are simple things like, you know, like you could show a picture of a guy with a string tied around his finger, you know. Here's a reminder, don't forget to attend your ETF webinar. Make sure you check your email inbox, right? Yeah. It's a little freaky. You got to, you know, you got to word it correctly. You kind of got to, yeah. People in my market, investors, they wouldn't, they wouldn't figure it out. They don't know. They would just think that I'm advertising everywhere. Really. Yeah. But the retargeting thing is huge because you're paying normal banner ad rates, essentially, for extremely targeted banners. Let's see. You know, the other ones, we talked about the ad server, we talked about retargeting, we talked about, you know, the main key in all this for the meeting by side of things is you've got to have something dedicated, somebody in your company dedicated to doing this. It's, you're not going to hit home runs on every single one. You know, a lot of times when we think about buying advertising, we think, well, I'm going to buy this ad today, and it's going to make me X amount of dollars. I'm going to buy this ad tomorrow, and it's going to make me X amount of dollars. Usually it's the Pareto principle. You're going to do it, you're going to buy, do 10 different things. Eight of them are going to totally flop, and two of them are going to make it all worthwhile. Right? Yeah, we got a question over here. I mean, even talking about hold networks, I have, you know, there's hold networks that I just can't make work. Like AdBright, I don't know, I just waste money there. Facebook, I personally have never made Facebook work. People do, I know, not me. On the retargeting, are you then creating new sets of banner ads, or is that being done within that network? No, you have to give them the banner ads. Right? So that's, you know, if you're going to display advertising, you really need to start keeping a swipe file of all the great banner ads that you see. You know, from my market, I go to sites like Newsmax.com, you know, all those conservative, Republican, right wing sites have all those financial, like, Agora type of sites, Weiss Research, MotleyFool.com, and I save all those banners. They're not making the banner with a text locked in. They're giving me a template that I can use, right? They're just putting filler text in for the headline, filler text in for the, and then I can adjust the banner to whatever I want. Yeah, that's MSN ad center. Ad center? Yes. That's what, it's a convergence now. It used to be, there used to be Yahoo small business ads. It's now merged with MSN. And that traffic is fabulous. I mean, it's only about a third of Google AdWords, but they're a lot nicer people to deal with. Thank you. Do you want to be these ads that work for anything? Do they target local markets or any of the retargeting? Will they do local? Yeah, retargeting. You can target by geographic area sometimes. And same thing with the ad networks. You can target, you know, the ad networks, they like that whole demographic feature. So yeah, you can definitely target just by area. You know, one thing that a lot of people do on, on, people used to do this on AdWords all the time is they'd find out, this is kind of a sneaky thing. They'd find out where the, the affiliate program owner lived, right? And then they just made sure they didn't show their ads in that locale. And then they would do like trademark bidding everywhere else in the world. But the, the affiliate program owner would check their, you know, they type in, well, let's see if somebody's using my trademark term type it in. No ads. I'm great. But it was only not showing up in Dallas or showing up everywhere else in the world. Yeah. There's a lot of sneaky things with, with ad networks. You know, with ad networks, you're going to be doing insertion orders, which are, you know, you're basically talking to a sales rep. And you're saying, here's what I'm agreeing to buy. And just like we talked about with solo ads, there's one line you want to try to get on the bottom of every single one, you know, what, what happens if this doesn't work? And that's, that's your make good. You know, they're not all going to give it to you. But by the simple fact of you asking for it up front, they know that you're a serious advertiser. You just want to prove to them that you already know your numbers. And if something doesn't work, it's most likely on their, their, their shoulders. I'm trying to think of other things to cover with ad networks. Can you think of anything, Russell, we didn't talk about. I mean, we kind of covered over the ad server stuff really quick and the networks. The nice thing about this market, there are people you can hire that that's all they do is media buying. So the way that a media buyer typically works is it actually doesn't cost you anything. They, they bill you and then they, you know, they take 15% turn the rest over to the, to the, to the advertiser. It's market dependent. I would just go to Google and type in the whatever your general market is. Like if I go type in investing media buyer, there's, there's a couple of names that will come up. And what you're looking for is you're looking for usually a small, small shop. You're not looking for a corporation that does media buying, right? That's just a bunch of employees that are, that's what they're assigned to do. You're looking for a guy or gal that that that's their expertise. And that's how they make their livelihood is, is being an expert in that field. Right. So there's a couple of them in the internet marketing, Drew Kosloff, I know, and you mentioned Davis. Let's see. Greg Davis. In internet marketing stuff. Doesn't do health stuff. I think Greg's busy up. Busy up stuff. Yep. Is there a side that sells better? Is there colors that work better in the ads? I'm too excited to show that. Is there a certain banner side? Yeah. I mean, when you're dealing with banner ads, there's two type of banners. Is there, do I just write in this with a pen, Russell? One of these? Yes. All right. So there's two types of banners. Okay. And then you're going to see great examples of landing pages. So you've got to motleyfool.com. Because you're on Motley Fool. It's kind of a new site. Well, if you click on that link, it's going to go to a page that looks like this. I have really bad handwriting. And that's all you're going to say. It says it has a headline. It has an email entry box and that is it. Go to Motley Fool and you'll find dozens of those. Okay. So that's a landing page. Now, a landing page like that you couldn't use on Google AdWords. And really, we don't use it much now that we use webinars because we go right for the registration. You can test that, you know, instead of going right for webinar registration, you're going to get about 25 to 30% off of cold traffic because that's what we got off of AdWords using that exact type of format. You want banners that stick out or banners that blend in. So if you go to Motley Fool, you're going to see banners that kind of blend in. There'll be something like this. They'll have like a little border and then, you know, headline and then a bunch of text and maybe a picture over here. And it's all very small text. It's not, you know, when we think of banners, we think of big flashy things with like one or two words. These are all very, very content heavy. I mean, and actually in most cases from what we do, there's more content, there's more writing on the banner ad than on the page we used to send them to. Right? So go to Motley Fool. Those are investing type of sites. And just start keeping a library of those because when it comes to banners, you're going to want to have a whole stable of different things you can test out, different things you can try. This is one of those ways that you can, once you have a process that works, I mean, in media buying, you could spend hundreds of thousands of dollars a day on just on banner ads. So you want to have a process that works, something that's kind of industrial strength. The way that I think about it, Russell's been talking the last couple of days a lot about the great thing about this automated webinar business is that you can get something that works. You can get something up and running and it works as is, right? But you can always tweak and add things and increase it. It really is an amazing, amazing thing. Well, while we've been sitting here today, I didn't add by this morning for about 2,500 bucks. And so far we've made about $8,000 off of it. So, I mean, just sales coming in while we're at lunch and that's not even without the evening webinars yet. So, but the cool thing is, and this is something I learned early on in my marketing career, I've had a lot more downs than Russell. I've had ups and downs, but we all have ups and downs, but I think I've had more than Russell. Maybe that's just personal. One thing that you learn very quickly in the internet marketing business is bad times create great systems and something that's very efficient. Good times breathe complacency. So if you think about when you have created your most optimized system, the system that works the best with the most amount of traffic and is the most efficient, it's always when you were kind of in a slump and you had to make it work, right? And then when things start going well, what do you do? Well, if you're a salesperson, you know, you kind of slack off, you know, in the beginning of the month rolls around and you got all the time in the world. Well, the key in this business is to continue to ratchet it up month after month after month. You know, when, you know, you got to continue ratcheting up what your EPCs are and how much you can afford to pay to get a registered. Because what that does in the ad network side of things is if say, you know, if I'm getting a $4 EPC, you know, maybe I can advertise on, oh, who knows, a thousand sites. What happens if I go from a $4 EPC to a $5 EPC? Now, this isn't scientific. This isn't mathematically proven. But I mean, my EPC only went up by 20%, right? But what happens to the number of sites that I can profitably advertise on? It goes up exponentially. It's probably going to be something like 2,000. You know, if I increase my EPC to $6, that number would probably go to 10,000. It goes up because now you can, your universe is broader that you can advertise to profitably. Because when it comes to sites to advertise on, there's really only a handful that are absolute slam dunk money makers. Then you have the vast majority of the also rands that, you know, they sell advertising. Some people make money. Some people don't make money. So they have this constant churn of advertisers. They're willing to be flexible if you can make it work. If all of a sudden, if you know your EPCs are $6, the world opens up to you. It's the whole concept. Jonathan Meisel actually was the first one to come up with this concept years and years ago. And the whole idea was, if your site converts well enough, eventually your competitors are going to go away and they're going to make more money just sending their traffic to you and taking their affiliate cut. Well, if you think about it, that is a very real possibility, right? If they can only make $3 EPC, and if you crank up your EPC to $7 EPC and you're paying 50%, all of a sudden they could just send all their traffic to you and make more money with only half the money. Right? So when you're dealing with these media buys, it's always kind of, you have to approach it from two sides. Yes, you can go out and buy the media, but you also have to remember, continue to ratchet up your webinar process. So, I mean, ratcheting up the webinar process, you know, Russell's talked about this a bunch, but some of it's just easy things. Right, I would go to Fiverr.com, F-I-V-E-R-R.com, and we're adding all kinds of humorous type of videos on our follow-up, because if you get a person to register for the webinar, right, there's different things that can happen. They can attend the webinar and buy, right? Attend the webinar and not buy, not attend the webinar and not buy, right? And that's the biggest market is attend the webinar, no, didn't attend the webinar and didn't buy. So we're just going to send them these random videos that are funny, that are kind of tied in, right? But to get them to watch the replay. So if you go to Fiverr, you can, for five bucks, that's why it's called Fiverr, you can look under rap. You can get a professional rap artist to create a rap about your product or service and send you a video of them doing this rap for five dollars. Or you can get them, you know, a kid on a surfboard to say your 12-word message as he surfs in the short. Just go to Fiverr and look around. I mean, just think of all the random and crazy things, especially in my market, because they don't know about Fiverr.com. They don't know they only cost me five bucks. They think, wow, that guy must really like that webinar. He created a rap video about it and sent it into CURT. I really need to watch that. So lots of different cool stuff. What is your testing process when you start out? Okay, well, my testing process is I always try to spend no more than a thousand dollars on any source to start out with. The only reason I picked a thousand dollars is because that's what I make from one sale. So I'm willing to risk a thousand dollars with the hopes that it returns, you know, at least that money back, a thousand bucks. And then I usually turn stuff off after, you know, a thousand or two thousand dollars go by. Initially, you know, I track everything almost daily, but after it's up and running and kind of optimized, I check it once a week. I'm trying to think. Is there any other media buying questions? They go straight to it's a three minute video that's attend my webinar, etc, etc. And we've done lots of tests on that. Our tests have concluded the shorter the video, the better. And if there's intro music, that's about a 10 to 12% bump in increasing response. So our video right now, the one that's won the last round of tests is about a minute, 12 seconds long on the registration page. Has a little intro music that kind of dies down and then I start talking. And it's not. I mean, I did it in screen flow in like 10 minutes. It's not professionally done. He was speaking about the book and about everything. And one thing he was talking about, he was talking about how forever people always talk about how important content is. And content is the biggest, the most important part of your business, right? And then he started talking about, I can't remember the exact status, something like, you look at all the content that was created from the history of time up until 1992 or whatever it was, all that content, I think it was replicated right now every two days. Some crazy date that, or every day, or whatever it is. Something crazy like that. I said, right now there's so much content out there, if your business is based off of content, you're going to drown, or you're going to die really fast. He said that the difference is like, what you need to understand is that what sells and what's going to make you stand out from the crowd is the context, it's the relationship. That is what's going to make people want to listen to your content, make people pay attention to yours, because there's so much stuff out there right now it's impossible to get through people. So he says it's about context. That's the reason why he goes and he speaks to these keynotes because he wants to talk to people and get the context and the relationship. It's why he's on social media, it's why he's doing all these type of things. And so me, that was a big epiphany for me because I don't really enjoy doing live events that aren't in Boise. I don't really like going and traveling all these places. I did a lot more of it last year than I wanted to. But what's interesting is like we built so many good relationships so many people by doing that. And there's that part of, this is my whole internal struggle of like the context versus you know, I don't really, I don't enjoy leaving and traveling but I enjoy the relationships and all the things that happen because of that. So I kind of am stuck in this spot. But we talked about, if you don't want to be out traveling you don't have the platform, you have those type of things. That's what social media is for, is that platform for you to start communicating and interacting and getting people where they can kind of feel you and become part with you at a personal level. And so for me, what we're basically gonna be focusing on really hard is three major social networks, Facebook, Facebook, LinkedIn and Twitter. And this is outside of obviously driving ads and all those other type of things. But my biggest thing is really to start building the context with people, communicating with people. Cause I kind of, you know, in my office I kind of shut myself in and you know, people can't call me because I get so many calls. So kind of like blocked out, but which is good from one standpoint cause you don't want to be spending all your time in a reactive mode like, you know, answering people's phone calls and questions. But there's part of it where you need to have that context, you need to have that relationship, the dialogue that's happening with people. So I'm gonna start kind of with a Facebook strategy that I'm gonna be focusing on. One thing, Dagan Smith told me that he was, he started doing his business. Question over here? Does it make sense to host your own forum? Yeah, the question was just make sense to host your own forum. Yeah, it also sucks really bad, just so you know. I've had like four or five forms in my life, and every one of them I've always shut down, because they always end up being a nightmare. We have Dotcom Secrets Local, which is the longest lasting forum we've ever had, but I've also got four people internally who man the forum every single day. We've got a couple moderators as well. So it makes a lot of sense, because then you're controlling the conversation, but just know ahead of time that it's not an easy task either. You get people coming in trying to spam stuff, you get people who think they use it for their customer support. I mean there's just a whole bunch of things like that. So just be aware ahead of time. If I was gonna do it again, and what we're doing right now with Dotcom Secrets Local is finding the people who are active in the forum right now, and kind of pulling them aside and asking them to become moderators, so that you're not personally moderating, because it'll eat up your entire existence if you allow it. But there's a lot of strategy behind that. Interactive forums, or? Yeah, interactive forums. Yeah, yeah, if no one's in there, then don't, you know. One thing that, a good strategy for launching a forum, if you guys watch what Tim Ferris did when he launched the four hour body, he took his existing customer list and said, hey, we're launching for our body, we're gonna be making a private members area, and he had people apply to become part of his private members area. He took, I don't know, 100, 200 people that applied, from his private members area, and started these conversations, got people talking, and got them conversating, and had this thing happening, and these guys were all like, they were pitching why I should be part of this forum, like a whole take away, so like this is why I should be part of your forum, this is why I should be in there. So they got in there, they wanted to prove that they should be in there, so they're commenting and adding much value, so then when he opened it to the floods, there were already three or four hundred people in there, had posted thousands of posts, all with huge value, all of them became my member moderators, so then when all these floods of people came in, they already had the platform there, and it just grew and expanded from there. So really smart strategy if you wanna launch a forum, I do something like that. But again, what I'd be focusing on, because it just blows my mind to that, how this was my whole business prior, and we used to spend probably an hour to two hours a day going through forum by forum trying to answer questions, trying to help as much as we could, and all we would do is have like, in our signature file, was a link back to our squeeze page, which now would be linked to my webinar, and you're never pitching, you're never selling, you're always trying to provide value, and you do that, people know really quickly, like who's legit, they can tell if you're legit really quick just by the value and the comments and the content you're providing. So I'd say my biggest thing for next year is blocking off your 20 or 30 minutes of my day, every single day where it's in my calendar, schedule that thing pops up, okay, boom, here's your 12 forms, go and see what kind of value you can provide so you can get that context. You know, it might be 20 minutes on Twitter, 15 minutes on Facebook, whatever it is, I'm gonna start chunking out my time, and having probably an hour a day where my whole goal is nothing to do with selling, nothing to do with ASF, it all has to do with context, like getting that relationship, because for me, I always forget about that part, I get so excited about the sales, the numbers, the metrics, and you forget sometimes that these little EPCs and the numbers are all tied and associated to people, and if you don't forget about that, again, that's when you start seeing the value. I mean, you were talking yesterday your list prior, it was mailing your list, and after you told them about your dad and everything, now they became people to you, and how much, I'm sure it's affected your bottom line, your business, as these become more people, and like the way you, everything you do changes, okay, the way you communicate with your audience, the way you share, the way you, everything just changes. When we used to do, for three years, we did a workshop in our office every quarter, we brought our customers in and did a whole, like a three day event, and it was cool, because we'd actually break up our office, and people meet with, they spend time with our copywriter, time with our SEO guy, time with everyone in our office, and I loved it because it got everyone in our office feeling the, having that context as well, it changed it from, this is a business, this is numbers to, these are actually people we're affecting, every single day we're here, we're doing something bigger, and it really affects your team and your company as well. So I'd recommend that from a social strategy, looking at Facebook, LinkedIn, Twitter, but then also finding out where the active forms are in your marketplace, whatever it is, and becoming part of those conversations, okay, it's the same equivalent like in a local business, going to the networking meetings, going to the chamber of commerce, going those type of things, because that's where the business owners are at, that's where you're meeting different people, that's where it opens up so many gates, networking is huge, and I've had four or five comments just in the last two days here about how good the networking just in this room has been, like how many different people have been here, how much value that you guys have got from that, okay. So it's like really, it's been really, it's been interesting to see the people that have been looking for me, because I guess since they've started this whole thing where people can go and subscribe to you, so if you Google people, and I'm always posting uplifting stuff, and so now all of a sudden I get these people who are absolutely looking for me and trying to connect with me, so it's been really cool. That's awesome. I just need to learn how to convert it. How to monetize it, that's awesome. Okay, so there's my social strategy, I'm gonna move on to classified ads. This is kind of a fun one for me, so I have a friend named Gil Ortega, anyone here who ever met Gil? None of you, okay, as go guys. Gil is awesome, he's one of my favorite people, and Gil probably, I can't remember, four or five years ago, he owned the biggest lead generation company in the network marketing industry. All he did is he generated leads, and he sold pretty much every network marketing company, Herbalife, Manatech, all these companies, he generated all these leads and sold them all to distributors. Millions and millions of leads they generate every single month. He ended up selling the company for crazy, $20 or $30 million, and then the people that bought it ran into the ground, he bought it back a year later for like almost nothing. So that was kind of a fun story for him. But I was talking to him, I'm like, how in the world, if I could generate 20 or 30 million leads a year, how do you do that? That's a lot of leads you generate really fast. And what he told me was completely backwards from what I would have guessed. He said, the most people that generate leads, doing typical stuff like we're talking about, doing SEO, doing paid search, stuff like that, but the biggest pool of people when you're generating leads, especially for a network marketing program, are classified ads. He said the typical, the typical classified ad site gets like a 1% landing some of the jobs. So one of the people who posts a resume on a help wanted site actually ends up getting a job. And he's 99% of the people who are on a help wanted site are what? They're angry. They're angry and upset because they hate their job, they hate their boss, they don't have a job, whatever it is. He said, that's where we started doing all of our marketing. He said, they're spending like $800,000 or $900,000 a month just going through and buying ads and scraping classified sites, craigslist, monster.com, all these different sites like that because that's where the business opportunity seekers are at. They're looking for something different. So when he told me that, I started having, obviously we sell business opportunities, I'm in like a million network marketing programs, I had kind of my epiphany like, wow, that's where a lot of these people are. Then a little while ago, I also had a chance to hear Greg Caesar speak. Later here, Greg Caesar speak before. He did a great presentation on classified ads and he basically told me the same thing after he said, he said, a lot of these guys, they generate all of their leads for their network marketing program through small, tiny classified ads. And so kind of another epiphany. And so we're doing a lot of things with classified ads now from kind of three different standpoints. I'm going to share all three of those. The first one, if you go to page number 86 right now, we just started running ads to our webinar through actual real classified ads. Like last week, if you had picked up, or two weeks ago, if you picked up a Thrifty Nickel anywhere in the country, every Thrifty Nickel in the country had my ad in for my automated webinar. Or you can buy segments, you can buy just the West Coast, or East Coast, or whatever you can buy smaller sections for, $50 bucks, $100 bucks, really cheap, to get a lot of coverage. Katie's a real person who works in my office. Well, when they answer the phone, I have tested it where when they answer the phone, I have Katie pick it up. Now, next thing for online classified ads, for those of you guys who are in .com Sears Local, we gave you guys all a really cool software program called Your Local Shortcut. And this, and it's a really cool tool that will do a lot of classified ad stuff. It also pulls yellow pages, it pulls the, we just had recently the UK version of the yellow pages, it scrapes data from there. Also source stuff like that. But one of the tools in there is it'll do stuff on Craigslist. So basically with Craigslist, there's two strategies. One Craigslist strategy, one Craigslist strategy, it's huge software, there's a lot of software programs out there to do right now. Your local shortcut does it, there's other ones out there as well. Basically, you type in, you put in a city, you put in a description, and it'll go to Craigslist, and it'll scrape all Craigslist, and it'll say, okay, someone is looking for an SEO job in Boise, Idaho, and it'll pull like, here's 10 companies looking for SEO job, or it could be people who posted a resume that say they're good at computers and pulls, everyone who's good at computers who's posted a resume in Boise. And it gives you all the data, it pulls out email address, and you can then email the people. So that's kind of one strategy we do a lot of. The pros and cons of that strategy, the pros is you get a lot of leads very, very quickly. The cons is there's gonna be a percentage of people who are upset about you when you contact them, because they're posting, you're looking for a job offer, and if you send them a business opportunity, there's gonna be some people that are upset. But it is very effective, and it works really good. So that's one thing, and then the other thing is just going out and posting ads on Craigslist, which works incredibly effectively, incredibly well, but because it works so well, Craigslist gets spammed so hard. They have so many rules and so many things to block people from just going and posting. You couldn't just go right out and post 30 ads on Craigslist by yourself. Like you would be shut down almost immediately. But when you do post ads, it gets posted. It's amazing the kind of results. We were doing it for a weight loss network marketing program. When we were posting one or two ads, and from those ads, or not, excuse me, one or two ads, we were posting 100 ads from 100 ads. I was paying these guys in, I think it was Pakistan, paying them a dollar per ad to post these ads. We were getting anywhere from 800 to 1,000 clicks for $100 worth of ads. So it was a great, like the EPCs were getting and it was amazing and the conversion was great on them. They were good people coming off Craigslist. And so those works really, really good, but the problem is it keeps getting harder and harder to post ads on Craigslist. So the good thing, there's a lot of different companies out there that do this. And the way that, I don't know, the way I always find these companies is I go, I told you guys earlier about Wicked Fire. Wicked Fire always has these guys from different countries like Pakistan or Uzbekistan or different places like that who are Craigslist posters. And the only place I could ever find these guys is on Wicked Fire. So if you go to Wicked Fire, create a free account and just search for Craigslist poster, you'll find people who are doing it. And it seems like guys will do it for a while then they stop doing it. So I always go there to try to find who's the newest guy, who's someone that's posting that they do it. And I've always found three or four different people that can do it. And right now, it used to be about a dollar per posted ad you would spend, which was amazing, was the greatest thing in the world. Right now, the cost has gone up because it's gotten harder for them. The whole system's difficult. You have to have a dedicated phone box for every single ad they post. So you guys have like 10,000 phone numbers that they're paying for. And then the posting ad is hooked at this number and they can't use that number again for 72 hours. And it's this big, huge thing. So it's a big type of company. It's a big deal to make these things happen. So right now, we're paying it a lot closer, about $3 an ad to be posted. But each ad, we're gonna make at least three clicks. We're getting at least a dollar if you see. But usually, we're getting a lot more from that from an ad. So that's kind of what you're looking at right now in today's market. It's about $3 per posted ad. And that's kind of how it works. So that's kind of what we're doing with our getting people posting. Pretty much all the guys that I know personally now are making money. You're doing postcards. He's their list broker, which is nice because he knows every single list they're buying at. Plus a lot of times you can buy all of his clients' lists as well. So he's been really good for us so far. So he's a good recommendation for any of you guys to do postcards. He'll get you the list. Basically, you create the postcard, you send it to him. He looks at the copy, and he goes and tries to match the list that are going to work for you. And he's been fantastic for us so far. You're pushing them to whatever the offer is. This is a very simple thing. Now, the next evolution and what we're testing after we get the postcards dial in is a concept called tear sheets. Anyone here know where a tear sheet is? OK, the first time I ever saw a tear sheet, I was just gotten married. I was living in the Rossie Street house. Everyone wants to drive around the downtown boys. You can find this little house that my wife and I were living in the top. And me and Josh were sharing an office in the basement apartment. And I got this thing in the mail. And it was like one of those wedding envelopes, like little bigger envelopes. So it stuck out kind of weird in the mail. And it was handwritten on. I opened it up. And there was this folded up news article. And there was a sticker on it. And it said, hey, check this out. Russell checks out from Jay. I thought it was from this kid named Jason Price, who were my buddies, who's kind of like in this whole make money thing with me. And so I pulled down this whole article about something in the mortgage industry. I can't even remember what it was about. But something like that. I read the whole article. And I was like, this is the coolest thing in the world. Because Jason sent to me. So it must have something really legitimate. And so that was kind of what happened. And then I didn't think too much about it after that. And then fast forward, like four years later, I was at a Dan Kennedy event. And this guy named Scott Tucker stood up and he started talking about tear sheets. And he's like, this is how it works. He talks about things. He said inside, we put a yellow sticky note on there. We've tested a whole bunch of different messages, the one that converts the best, to saying, hey, check this out. From Jay, it's like everyone knows someone that starts the name Jay. And I was just like, holy crap, I saw this thing happen. This guy's doing it. And then what was most amazing to me, and maybe it was a closing technique, but it freaking closed me. I was sprinting in the back of the room. He had a whole bunch of his clients in there. He said, OK, I want you guys standing up. He had these people come up and said, OK, tell us what you were doing when you were mailing postcards. The guy said, I was mailing postcards that was, again, three to one response. What happened when you switched to tear sheets? Switched to tear sheets, they got a 45 to one response. Boom, next guy. Next guy. I had probably 20 to 25 people in a row sharing the same results. Went from postcards to tear sheets. These response went from two to three times profit to like a 30 times. And just crazy. So we bought it. We did our very first tear sheet. This is a couple years back. And I have a picture of it right here on page 89 and page 90. And Kevin actually was the one to roll the copy for this. We had so much fun with this. Basically, we wanted to make an article talking about all of the different people scamming you online. And basically, write an article about that and how we had this free DVD teaching where you could actually make money online. And so we wrote the whole article. And there's a front page there. And then on the back page, you see we have basically just testimonials of our students in there kind of reconfirming what we were talking about. Kind of funny things on the side. One thing that we learned is you want to get things that are going to get people's heads to pot. You'll get people to read the thing. So you look on page 89 on the right hand side. I went to iStock Photo. And I told Kevin to find the hottest girl he could find in iStock Photo. And that was the girl he found. And she was pretty cute. And then he wrote this little fake article. They used half of it cut off. And then at the same time, this was right when the rich jerk was doing his Playboy Mansion party. And it was like two or three months before that. So we made this little fake article about rich jerk's Playboy Mansion party turns out to be a flop. Playboy Bunny's no show after Outrage. And Kevin wrote that. So anyway, so those are the bulk of the traffic sources. Obviously, a lot of these we covered more of a fast thing. My goal at this event was to get you guys to see all the different ways that are out there. And again, some of these things are going to be really interesting to you, and some of these aren't going to be as interesting as to you. Some of you guys may never want to do offline stuff, other you guys are like, that's awesome. I'm going to start jumping into the offline stuff. Some of you guys may more like the SEO strategies, but pick the things that are really interesting to you, and then become students of each of those different things. And you become a student in a couple different ways. There's people you can learn from online, but they're really going to become a student who's jumping in and just trying it out. Kurt was talking about he jumps in these ad networks, he puts up $1,000 and sees what happens with it. And a lot of times that's a scary thing. It can be a lot of money. So test out things that are smaller, test out solo ads, things that are lower risk, things that are cheaper cost, and you make some money, reinvest that money back into it, reinvest that money back into it. When I interviewed Vince James, he made $100 million in 23 months. He told me the whole story. He said he was living in his parents' basement. He had no money. He said he had a pad of paper and a big pen. He had an idea, and he wrote out a sales letter with his big pen, and then took that letter, mailed it out. He read it, I think, one or two lists cost him $5,000. He mailed it out. The money that came back in, he took that money, reinvested it, reinvested it, reinvested it. And 23 months later, he made $100 million, $45 million cash into his bank account in 23 months. So look at this business like that. It's not something that you're going to make a million dollars overnight. But it's all about testing out small and seeing something work, take that money, reinvest it, and do it a little bigger. And then reinvest that, do it a little bit bigger. Reinvest it a little bigger. And pretty quick, in two or three cycles, you can start making a lot of money. So that's what I want you guys to look at in this business, is that's kind of how to do it. Do you guys have any questions about any of the traffic stuff we cover? I know we covered a lot of things. We have microphones, got some questions up here. Have you tried or do you have any resources for putting ads in products that are being shipped? I don't even know what that's called. I met a dude like five years ago. That's what he did. And I can't remember his name. I saw him one time at an event. He told me that's what he does. He's with, what's the big state company? It's shipped out of Kansas City, states, or Omaha, states, whatever. And that's what he did. He would put inserts into those boxes for offers. That was his whole business. He was killing it. And I never saw the guy after that. But yeah, so no, I'm useless. But it does work. I've seen somebody that actually made it work. So I don't know. That's an interesting thing. I've heard of people doing it where they find partners in their same industry who are shipping out products and will pay to put inserts into their products that are being shipped out as well, which is probably a lot smarter in a strategic move because you're not sending out to everyone who wants steak. You're sending out to people who are actually in your marketplace. But yeah, that would work really good. But I've never done it. Yeah, other questions right here? I noticed on these tear sheets, you've got scamfreedvd.com as a URL. Did you create a separate URL just to test these to track your conversion? Yeah. Yeah, we did a lot. Like we got thousands of URLs like that. So when we did our ad in the Classified Ads, I think we bought dclosloknow.com. And that was the ad we ran in all the Classified Ads. And when you click on that, it just goes back to our tracking link and redirects back to the actual webinar. So we only pull the stats. We can see exactly how many clicks came from that ad. So a lot of times, especially when you're doing print media, you don't want to have hypertracker.com slash 123 slash 1845. No one's going to actually go and look in the Classified Ad, try to remember to type in. You want something short and concise and related. So for $8 a year, it's worth that. And then you can run those ads. And especially if you're in the Classified Ad, you're running an ad, it's going to be out there. And then two weeks later, that ad's dead. And you can reuse that URL again another time, because it's not like an evergreen thing when things are always happening. You look at a lot of the infomercials that are on TV right now, the two minute infomercial spots and 30 second infomercial spots. They'll have the infomercial on the very bottom. It's like a black line. There's a URL in there. And they're always like, make cash, 6942.com, or something like that. And every single media outlet they're running that ad, and that URL is just changing. But there's some quick trackers like that to be able to track it really fast. So I would definitely go and invest in 10 or 20 or 30 short domains that are related to your product that you can use for anything print related like that. Good question. Any other questions at all? OK. My big question, I guess, is do you have a rule of thumb on your pre-frame when you will send somebody to a pre-frame site versus the login or the opt-in to your webinar? Opt-in. For the most part, I said most of my people straight to the webinar. It sounds like Kurt does as well. The times where we're not right now is the guy that's buying most of our media right now, he's pushing them all through the pre-frame page. And that's what he's had the most success with and he's really good at. I think a lot of times if you're doing your test, your $1,000 test, $5,000 test, try one way. Maybe even split the traffic between the two and see which ones end up converting. Having a visitor one go to the pre-frame, visitor two opt-in back and forth. So yeah. I don't think there's really a hard set rule. It's kind of just test each thing and see which ones work the better. And again, a lot of times, just people you hire, some people are going to feel better with one way. They're more familiar with one way. And so a lot of times, what do you recommend? What's worked for you in the past and have them do it that way? Let's give you a say on one thing that people often overlook. People often overlook when something needs to be positioned. So if what you're doing is on top of mind. You need to position it before he sends them to his webinar. So that's an obvious one where probably if they don't know an ETF as he's not their market. But however, that's a step that people often overlook when they're trying to convert traffic. Yeah, I love that. Awesome. Cool. Question over here? I'd like to revisit the blog post for a minute. OK. Yeah, are you guys proud of me for putting this book together? We all be lost without it. Woo! Yeah. All right. To end with, I just want to kind of, one thing I want to talk about before we end is basically actually two things. The first thing is, for everybody who came to Boise, one thing I promise to you guys is we're going to actually be starting in January. We're going to start documenting day by day what we're doing with our webinars. You guys can kind of see behind the scenes. So hey, we just bought this ad. The results, the click through. So that'll be kind of a really fun thing. We're excited for that. Second thing is, I know at any kind of event like this, you get so much stuff, you feel kind of overwhelmed. And so I want to just kind of give you guys what I do when I go to events that helps me to get things done when I get back home. Because that's always the hardest thing. You get back and you look at this book and you're like, huh, where do I start now? So the first thing I want to talk about is there's a concept. Somebody has probably heard me talk about this before. I heard a talk, though, between Gary Halbert and Michael Fortin, talking about, they call the concept letter gold. Have you ever heard of this story before? So he says that down in Mexico, where the drug cartels, and they want a law change, a law pass, they go to the Mexican government and say, hey, we need this law to pass. And they're like, no, we're not going to pass that law. For you, that's crazy. There's no way I'm going to do this. And so what they do that, again, the concept is called letter gold. They said, what happens is the mafia people break into the government officials' houses at night, and they walk up to them in their bed, and they walk up to them, and they say, hey, wake up right now. You've got two options. You're going to change this law, and I'm going to give you this bag of gold, or you're not going to change the law. I'm going to kill you. So your two options are letter gold. Which one is it going to be? That's the option. Now, that's your two choices. How hard is it for you to make a decision and get something done? That's your two options. It's very quick to get that thing to happen, right? So for me, when I first heard that story, I was like, he then went on to talk about how we need to trick our brains to put ourselves into a letter gold type situation. Most of us don't do that. Most of us, we kind of move around, and it would be nice to get this done, and we want to do this, but we never trick our brains into getting ourselves into a letter gold situation. And so he talked about, Gary Halbert was talking about, when he first wrote the sales letter for the Code of Arms, he has never heard that story before. He was writing this letter, and he sat down and said, if I had to get the sales letter delivered, and someone had to open it, had to read it, had to buy, what would I do? What would I do different than what everyone else has been doing? So everyone else right now, they're copying the envelope, like open inside, they headline copy on the envelope, and trying to get people open. Now if I had, if my life, my family's life, depended on this envelope getting delivered, what would I do different? He said, well, first off, I probably would make sure of his hand address. I wouldn't just put, here's a sticker of the person there, I'd hand address it, because that way it would seem real. And secondly, I probably wouldn't put a stamp on straight. I'd probably put the stamp on crooked, because it seemed like it was actually a real letter. And third off, I'd probably wouldn't put my return address, I'd probably just leave that off. And he started going through all these things he would do if he had to get that letter developed. So he would put himself in that mindset, and he created this whole campaign, wrote a whole sales letter based on this thing, started mailing it, and they mailed that letter hundreds of millions of times, became the most mailed sales letter in the history of time, made him hundreds of millions of dollars. And that whole thing came out of him putting himself in the mindset of, what would I have to do if my life depended on this type of thing? And I would really start thinking about, we need to start putting ourselves more into those type of things, more of that type of situation, otherwise things never get done. For those of you guys who ever launched a product, tell me this is not familiar. It doesn't matter if you're launching tomorrow or six months from now, it doesn't matter when you're going to launch. Every single time, the day before launch, you're going to be up in the office with your entire staff for like six in the morning trying to get everything done. Is that not correct? Doesn't matter how long you preface it, you're always there, and you never get to think, you always think it's done the second that it goes live. Every single time, it's weird how it always happens. It doesn't matter how much time you prep. So that means that if I'm going to launch a product in six weeks or six months, it doesn't really matter. I'm going to be up until midnight night before trying to get it done. So why don't I set that launch date six weeks from now or two weeks from now or tomorrow? And so what I started doing when I go to events like this, I come back, and when I usually I walk on the flight home, I start looking at this whole manual. Look at all this crap we learned. There's so much stuff. And I say, OK, if I'm going to do this, I need a timeline. And I come back and I say, OK, here's my timeline. And what is the very first thing we need to do? If you're going to eat an elephant, how do you do it? One bite at a time, right? So what's the very first bite? This is my whole automated webinar I got to launch and make a million dollars with. What's the very first step? The very first step is I have no idea what I'm going to sell. So I got to figure out what am I going to sell. I don't have a timeline that out. And I say, OK, now that's my very first thing. Now I need to set a letter goal date for that topic, that thing right there. So by Wednesday, I need to make sure that I know exactly what that is I'm going to sell. It's not like it'd be nice to have it done by then. This is my letter goal date. Either I'm going to die or I'm going to make money. That's my two options right now. And that has to be done by Wednesday. The next thing is I need to make sure I get this part done. And I get this part done. And I map out a timeline of every single thing that I'm going to be doing. And I come back and I assign a date to every single one of those things. I call my letter goal date. Lead or gold. I try to trick my mind into knowing that that is going to happen. I go back to my Outlook calendar. I put those things in. And once something in my Outlook calendar, it never budges. In fact, it's a lot of times it gets me in trouble because I'll have something happening. But I have something in my calendar. It's stuck in my calendar. It's there. I have to do it. So I'll pull them all night. Or whatever it takes to make sure I hit that thing on my calendar. I try to make sure I'm not really flexible with my calendars. Because the worst thing you can do is be a kind of person like, I missed that start. I'll just move it to Wednesday. We'll shift all this stuff off and boom. Now we're six months later. If you want to be successful, this is the way you do it. You've got to trick your mind into making sure that you make and you keep and you set those deadlines and you hit them. Because I promise you, it doesn't matter if you set your deadline tomorrow or six months from now, you're going to hit it. Not until you set that deadline. So that's the right plan for you guys. Is go back to your flight's home or whatever it is. Make sure you sit down for an hour or so and just go through all the stuff and say, what is the step-by-step process? You've learned everything. You've been dumped as much stuff as we could on you. But what is the step-by-step process? Go back through, timeline it out, set realistic deadlines for everything, one of those things, and stick to it no matter what. And if you do that, that's how you're going to be successful with this. That's what I've done over and over and over again. It's the only reason why we were able to get our webinar launched and we did it. The only reason why we got it off the ground, why everything worked, because we set those deadlines, we stuck to it as a team, and we were able to roll it out and launch it. And then something happens after it's launched. Then come back and start sending income goals. I mean, my initial goal is we want to make $10,000 a day. We got there really, really fast. And I'm like, oh, sweet. And I've been happy in a good spot with that. And after going to Mexico when he told me he's making $100,000 a day, I was pissed. I'm like, freak. So now I got to come back and I got to set all my different benchmarks. By January 31st, I need to make sure we're at $20,000 a month. And then $30,000. And then I'm going to come back and set all these different dates for our team and say, these are what we've got to be hitting. We need to be pushing ourselves to those dates. And when you do that, it's amazing what happens. It's amazing what mountains move and all those type of things. I'm not big in selling personal development stuff. Law of attraction, all that kind of stuff. I don't care if you believe it or not. There's something to all that kind of stuff. And you put things like this out there, weird things start happening. Connections start being made. People you start meeting when you're out there and you're actively moving towards a goal. And those who don't do this kind of thing, those people that you never hear from again. I want to hear from all you guys. I want to make sure your webinar is live. I want you guys putting your webinar in our network. We want you guys making a lot of money. But that's our long-term goal with this. And so make sure you're doing these things. And anything we can do along the way to help facilitate, let us know as well. Because we obviously have the best interest in all of you guys as well. We want to make sure you're successful as well. So other than that, thank you guys so much for coming out here and sitting through this for two days. I hope you had a great time. Give yourselves a round of applause for everything. Thank you.
